chunk_summary: Guidelines for risk-tiered lab monitoring and action thresholds in preterm/LBW/ELBW infants post-discharge, focusing on biochemical markers, iron status, bone health, and renal function, with specific numeric cutoffs and targeted imaging recommendations.
learning_objectives: Understand the timing and frequency of lab monitoring for preterm/LBW/ELBW infants post-discharge.
Identify key biochemical markers and their thresholds for iron deficiency, bone health, and renal function.
Learn when to initiate targeted imaging based on lab results and clinical suspicion.
Recognize risk factors requiring earlier or more frequent monitoring, such as diuretics, cholestasis, or hypercaloric feeds.
key_facts: Biochemical markers should be reviewed around 1 month post-discharge, with earlier checks for high-risk infants.
Ferritin <35–40 µg/L suggests iron deficiency, while >300 µg/L indicates overload (absent inflammation or liver disease).
Bone health markers: ALP >900 IU/L with phosphorus <4.6 mg/dL indicate low bone mineral density.
Electrolytes with urea/creatinine are essential for infants on hypercaloric feeds or prolonged diuretics.
Serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D monitoring is recommended in cholestasis or high-dose supplementation cases.
Targeted imaging includes bone radiographs for biochemical abnormalities or fractures and renal ultrasounds for nephrocalcinosis risk.
reference_quote: A practical timing anchor from the ABM protocol framework is to review biochemical markers around 1 month after discharge (and again later in infancy if risk persists).
document_key_topics: Risk-tiered lab monitoring for preterm/LBW/ELBW infants
Biochemical markers and thresholds for iron and bone health
Targeted imaging for bone and renal abnormalities
